nativeRange.habitat: Standing or slow-moving freshwater — ponds, oxbow lakes, ditches, marshes, and the quiet margins of slow rivers; floats freely on the surface where there is full or partial sun and humid air

decorPreferences: open, calm water surface for floating mats
low to moderate surface agitation — strong flow or spray bars break up the velvety leaf coating
open-top or rim-only lid to allow leaves to stay above the waterline

goodWith: live-bearing fish fry (provides shade and shelter)
labyrinth fish (gouramis, bettas) that use surface cover for bubble-nesting
avoidWith: tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars, or persistent overhead drip that wets the leaves

summary: Common Salvinia (Salvinia natans) is a small floating aquatic fern whose paired, velvet-textured leaves spread quickly across the water surface, forming a mat that shades the tank, suppresses algae, and shelters fry and shrimp with very little effort from the keeper.
careNotes: Common Salvinia is one of a handful of fully aquatic ferns kept in the aquarium hobby. Each plant carries two velvety, kidney-shaped floating leaves and a third submerged, finely-divided leaf that hangs below and functions as a root analog — the genus has no true roots, and all nutrient uptake happens through the leaf surfaces. The upper leaves are covered in tiny eggbeater-shaped trichomes that trap a thin layer of air, making the leaves strongly hydrophobic; persistently wet leaves (from spray bars, surface drip, or splashing filter returns) will rot at the base. Husbandry is otherwise undemanding: low to moderate light is sufficient, no CO2 is needed, fertilization can stay light, and a temperature range of 18–28 °C covers the typical community-tank window. Many sources recommend keeping temperatures toward the warmer end of that range for optimal growth, but S. natans is notably cold-tolerant for a member of the Salviniaceae and can be kept outdoors in temperate ponds through summer; in cooler climates it tends to die back in winter rather than overwintering as a live plant, regenerating from spores or stored fragments the following season. Growth rate is fast — biomass can double in a matter of days under good conditions — so regular thinning is needed to prevent the mat from sealing the surface and starving submerged plants of light.
breedingNotes: Common Salvinia propagates almost exclusively by vegetative fragmentation in the aquarium — breaking a healthy mat into smaller pieces is enough, as each piece continues growing from the lateral nodes. Spore production does occur under outdoor pond conditions where seasonal temperature swings trigger sporocarp development, and this is the route by which the species persists through winter in temperate climates, but spore-based propagation is not practical in a heated tropical aquarium. Like other members of the Salviniaceae, S. natans is heterosporous, meaning it produces two distinct types of spore structure (microsporocarps and megasporocarps) rather than a single kind.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the supplied Tropica plantdetails URL (/4767/4767) actually returns Salvinia minima, not S. natans — Tropica does not list S. natans in its catalog. Buce Plant page is product-specific to S. natans and provides pH 6.5-7.5, light low to high, CO2 not necessary, growth rate fast, size 2-3 inches, propagation by side shoots, placement floating, care difficulty easy.

additional.name: Bantam.earth
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Salviniaceae), native range (central Europe), pH (6.5-7.5), placement (surface; roots submerged, leaves in open air), propagation (division and spores on shoot tips), and leaf size (~0.5-1 inch). Lists temperature 60-70 °F (15-21 °C) — cooler than other sources, reflecting temperate vivarium use.

additional.name: Fishkeepingadventure.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (20-28 °C with tolerance to 15 °C in temperate zones), pH (6.0-7.5), growth rate (extremely rapid, biomass doubling possible in days), native range (central/eastern Europe, Asia, parts of Africa), propagation (spore reproduction plus vegetative fragmentation), and cold tolerance caveat.

additional.name: Aqualandpetsplus.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (20-30 °C optimal), pH (6.5-7.5), gH (4-15 dGH) and kH (3-8 dKH) — the only source giving hardness ranges. Confirms fast growth via stolons and limited tolerance to drops below 20 °C without dormancy.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Salvinia natans
additional.notes: Taxonomy reference: family Salviniaceae, order Salviniales, division Polypodiophyta. Confirms broad Eurasian + North African native distribution (Algeria, Egypt, Morocco, France, Germany, Ukraine, China, Japan, Taiwan, etc.), IUCN Least Concern, and naturalized/introduced status in parts of the northeastern United States.

propagation: sporocarp / spore production (mainly under outdoor pond conditions)
